creator: mat@FreeBSD.org
text:
+--On 7 mai 2014 15:18:33 +0100 Matthew Seaman <matthew@freebsd.org> wrote:
| On 05/07/14 15:07, mat@FreeBSD.org wrote:
|> Synopsis: dns/bind99 -- WITH_OPENSSL_PORTS and chroot failure
|> 
|> State-Changed-From-To: open->closed
|> State-Changed-By: mat
|> State-Changed-When: Wed May 7 14:03:31 UTC 2014
|> State-Changed-Why: 
|> GOST is compatible with chroot.  You do have to manually run:
|> 
|> mkdir -p $CHROOT/usr/local/lib/engines
|> cp /usr/local/lib/engines/*.so $CHROOT/usr/local/lib/engines
|> 
|> (Also, note that running BIND in a chroot is not supported on 10 for
|> that exact reason.)
|> 
|> http://www.freebsd.org/cgi/query-pr.cgi?pr=189420
| 
| I have to apply this manual fix every time openssl gets updated, which
| is not ideal.

Well, yes, tell me about it... On the other hand, you don't have to do that
each time, I don't think the gost engine has changed much since it was
committed...

You could also do a less stupid than what I just said thing a do a null
mount, put this in your fstab:

/usr/local/lib/engines  /var/named/usr/local/lib/engines nullfs ro 0 0

| Also, I know chroot is not supported on 10.x -- this took place on a
| bunch of 9.x servers, where chrooting is still supported.

It is, but I can't patch the /etc/rc.d/named to fix it, so...

-- 
Mathieu Arnold
